Eliphaz speaks with the voice of conventional wisdom, and what he says about God is true—but he speaks it to a man whose life has become a question that his theology cannot answer. He begins by mocking Job's appeals: who will answer your cries? Which of the holy ones will listen? Then he launches into a catalog of the fool's fate—his house cursed, his children defenseless, his wealth consumed by the hungry. The implication is unmistakable: Job, you must be the fool. Your suffering is the proof. Yet notice what Eliphaz claims to have merely *seen*—the fool taking root and then suddenly cursed. He speaks from observation, from what appears orderly and just in the world. What he cannot see is the complication that Job embodies: a righteous man suffering, a broken piece in the neat system he describes.

The heart of Eliphaz's counsel arrives in verse 8: "If I were you, I would appeal to God." He then paints a magnificent portrait of who God is—the One who does great and unsearchable things, who gives rain and lifts up the lowly, who thwarts the schemes of the crafty and catches the wise in their own cunning. None of this is false. God is indeed the sovereign redeemer who saves the needy and gives the poor hope. But Eliphaz makes a dangerous leap: he assumes that because God is powerful and good, Job's suffering must be corrective, and therefore Job must repent. He cannot imagine that God permits righteous suffering that serves purposes beyond correction—that sometimes the innocent suffer precisely because they are innocent, to display the sustaining grace of God rather than the consequences of foolishness.

When Eliphaz promises that the man whom God corrects will be blessed—wounded by God's hand but healed by it, rescued from six calamities, preserved through famine and battle, laughing at destruction—he is describing a world where suffering is always purposeful in a way that can be discerned and overcome. He guarantees that if Job turns to God, God will restore his tent, his offspring, his vigor. He has investigated this, he assures Job. It is true. But Job knows something Eliphaz does not: sometimes the investigation of a pious heart and the investigation of the actual world do not align. Sometimes a man can appeal to God, acknowledge His greatness, and still find his children dead, his flesh infected, and his friends insisting that his faith is not yet deep enough. The question that haunts this chapter is whether God's goodness can be trusted when the promises of the pious go unfulfilled—whether we worship God for what He does or for who He is.
